coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of ZnO photocatalyst composited carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from lime (Citrus aurantifolia)
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Hidayat, Rizky Nur
Widiyandari, Hendri
Parasdila, Hanaiyah
Prilita, Orien
Astuti, Yayuk
Mufti, Nandang
Ogi, Takashi
coredata.dc:description: Lime (Citrus aurantifolia) is one of the most extensively exported agricultural products from Indonesia. Citric acid and ascorbic acid content from limes (Citrus aurantifolia) is such one of the carbon-rich fruits that is a suitable source for Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) synthesis. CQD synthesized by relying on natural carbon precursors from lime (Citrus aurantifolia) is a more environmentally friendly approach in the present research, CQDs in this research will be used as an addition to ZnO by hydrothermal method in order to enhance photocatalytic activity. Corresponding to the results, the presence of CQDs improves both the optical and structural properties of ZnO, which led to enhance photocatalytic activity with a degradation percentage of 98% shortly after 75 min of visible light irradiation and to each of the repeatability test cycle results showing the consistent results and with the rate constant (k) fluctuating and the specified compound's degradation percentage remains relatively constant with minor changes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnO
CDQs
Green synthesis
Hydrothermal
Photocatalytic activity

The Author (HW) thanks to Osi Arutanti (National Research and Innovation Agency) for providing HR-TEM characterization.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis spectra of CQDs and (b) Digital photo of CQDs solution in an open space (left) and under UV (365 nm) irradiation (right).
(a) XRD patterns of ZnO and ZnO/CQDs and (b) FTIR spectra of ZnO and ZnO/CQDs.
Morphology sample of, (a) ZnO, (b) ZnO/CQDs, (c – e) element mapping of ZnO/CQDs, and (f) HRTEM image of ZnO/CQDs.

(a) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms, (b) dV/dD pore volume vs pore diameter adsorption process and, (c) dV/dD pore volume vs pore diameter desorption process, (d) PL spectra, and (e) Tauc plots of band gap energy.
Photodegradation comparison of ZnO and ZnO/CQDs of MB (a) C/C0 (b) lnC0/C, Ciprofloxacim (c) C/C0 and (d) lnC0/C, (e) Digital photo of MB solution after photodegradation reaction using ZnO/CQDs.
(a) Repeatability of ZnO/CQDs sample for the photodegradation of MB and (b) the kinetics of the reaction rate for each repetition of the photocatalytic activity test.
(a) Trapping experiments of active species and (b) Mechanism of charge carriers in ZnO/CQDs.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of ZnO photocatalyst composited carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from lime (Citrus aurantifolia)

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Mathematics and Natural Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lime (Citrus aurantifolia) is one of the most extensively exported agricultural products from Indonesia. Citric acid and ascorbic acid content from limes (Citrus aurantifolia) is such one of the carbon-rich fruits that is a suitable source for Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) synthesis. CQD synthesized by relying on natural carbon precursors from lime (Citrus aurantifolia) is a more environmentally friendly approach in the present research, CQDs in this research will be used as an addition to ZnO by hydrothermal method in order to enhance photocatalytic activity. Corresponding to the results, the presence of CQDs improves both the optical and structural properties of ZnO, which led to enhance photocatalytic activity with a degradation percentage of 98% shortly after 75 min of visible light irradiation and to each of the repeatability test cycle results showing the consistent results and with the rate constant (k) fluctuating and the specified compound's degradation percentage remains relatively constant with minor changes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Rapid industrialization has resulted in the various organic pollutants into the environment, notably water pollution, which has recently become critical issue concerning ecosystem balance [1,2]. Considering the current global energy and environmental issues, semiconductor ZnO photocatalysis has gained attention as a potential solution. ZnO is classified as a type-n semiconductor with a bandgap energy of 3.37 eV [3]. Its advantages include strong oxidizing ability, stable chemical and physical properties, biocompatibility, non-toxicity, high photosensitivity, and wide availability [4]. However, ZnO photocatalysts have limitations, such as low visible light photoactivity due to charge carrier recombination and low quantum efficiency [5].

One approach to address these challenges is reducing the bandgap energy and particle size to enhance photocatalytic efficiency by ZnO-composited with carbon-based such as: graphene, carbon nanotube (CNTs), carbon nanofiber (CF), and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [6]. ZnO-composited with CQDs as nanomaterials show great promise as co-catalysts for semiconductors [7]. Especially when it comes to their advantages which include; simple and cost-effective synthesis, low toxicity, excellent optical and catalytic properties, good chemical stability, biocompatibility, and high electrical conductivity [8,9]. CQDs also possess strong electron transfer properties, reducing the recombination of electron and hole charges, thereby compensating for ZnO's drawbacks [10,11].

In recent years, green synthesis, particularly the production of CQDs from plants, has gained significant attention due to its high stability [12]. Green CQDs synthesis involves the use of natural sources, such as plant extracts, as starting materials or reducing agents, instead of toxic chemicals [13,14]. One such carbon-rich fruit derived from limes (Citrus aurantifolia) is a suitable source for CQDs synthesis, primarily due to its citric acid and ascorbic acid content [15]. Lime (Citrus aurantifolia) is one of the most extensively exported agricultural products from Indonesia. According to data from the Central Statistics Agency, in 2019, Indonesia exported approximately 236 thousand tons of limes valued at around USD 40 million. Thus, the purpose of this study is to develop ZnO composited CQDs from natural resource which is lime (Citrus aurantifolia) for organic pollutant degradation materials with a visible-light photocatalyst as solution to the issue of environmental pollution.

ZnO were synthesized using analytical-grade Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (Sigma-Aldrich, Germany), C6H5Na3O7·2H2O (Merck, Germany), and NaOH (Merck, Germany) without any further purification. For ZnO preparation, 3.72 g of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O and 1.47 g of C6H5Na3O7·2H2O were dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water. The 1 M NaOH solution was added dropwise to achieve a pH of 13, followed by vigorous stirring for 100 min at room temperature. The mixture was then subjected to hydrothermal treatment at 130 °C for 10 h in autoclave and the residue was dried at 100 °C for 4 h. Pure ZnO was obtained by calcination at 500 °C for 3 h with a heating rate of 5 °C/min.

For the CQDs preparation, 20 mL of lime juice was dissolved in 20 mL of distilled water with stirring for 10 min. The solution was then transferred to the autoclave and subjected to a hydrothermal process at 180 °C for 6 h. A dark brown CQDs solution was obtained, and it was filtered to remove impurities before being mixed with ZnO. To prepare ZnO/CQDs photocatalyst, 0.2 g of ZnO was dispersed in 45 mL of H2O, and 1.5 mL of CQDs solution was added with continued stirring for 2 h. The mixture was then transferred to autoclave at 100 °C for 8 h and dried at 100 °C for 4 h.

X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance ECO equipped with Cu K radiation: λ = 1.5406) in the 2 range from 20 to 80 was used to explore the crystallographic properties of the final products. To investigate the functional group, the Fourier Transmission Infrared (FTIR, Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS5) was used. The surface morphology was observed using Field Emmision Scanning Electron Microscope/Energy Dispersive X-ray (FE-SEM/EDX, JEOL JIB-4610F). The UV–Vis diffuse reflectance (MAYA Pro2000 Ocean Optics) was used to determine the energy band gap. To investigate electron-hole recombination, photoluminescence (PerkinElmer LS 55) was used. The absorbance of MB solution before and after photocatalyst and irradiation, was measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi UH5300). To assess the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area and porosity parameters were performed at 77 K on a Micromeritics Tristar II Plus 3020 equipment.

The photocatalytic activity of ZnO/CQDs nanoflower was investigated utilizing photodegradation of a 10 ppm Methylene Blue solution in visible light (solar simulator, Peccel/PEC-L01 with the wavelength around 400–1100 nm). The lamp's distance from the sample had been adjusted to 11 cm. A 105-min test was performed on each sample, which consisted of 0.1 g of photocatalyst mixed with 50 mL of Methylene Blue solution. The first 30 min were taken up in complete darkness, followed by 75 min of visible light exposure. The samples were centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 30 min after photodegradation to separate the photocatalyst powder from the decolorized Methylene Blue (MB) solution and degraded ciprofloxacin.

The active species produced during the photocatalytic process were investigated by scavenger tests. To remove hydroxyl radicals (·OH), photoinduced holes (h+), and superoxide radicals (·O2-), scavengers such as iso propanol (IPA), ammonium oxalate (AO), and benzoquinone (BQ) were added to the solution, respectively.

Fig. 1(a) demonstrates that absorbance peaks of the CQDs solution are detected at the wavelengths of 227 nm and 279 nm which indicating π-π* transitions of CC and CC bonds, originating from the sp2 carbon core bonds and electron transitions n-π* from the CO and CO-H groups occur due to the successful oxidation of carbon-based nanoparticles and the presence of functional oxygen groups respectively [16]. Fig. 1(b) shows a brown-colored CQDs solution when placed in an open space and depicts the color of CQDs solution in the dark and under UV (365 nm) irradiation, indicating a color change from brown to blue.

Fig. 2 (a) shows the photocatalytic materials' XRD patterns. These results indicate that the photocatalytic material exhibited a hexagonal (wurtzite) crystal structure that is in line with the ZnO compound listed in JCPDS No. 36–1451. As a result, Table SI-1 shows that ZnO/CQDs have smaller crystallite sizes than ZnO. With the addition of CQDs nanoparticles to the ZnO crystal structure, the strain value and dislocation density increased significantly. This size difference could contribute to strain in the ZnO crystal structure when CQDs were added. Increased dislocation density has the potential to hinder charge recombination by inhibiting the mobility or accessibility of charge carriers [17]. The FTIR spectra can be observed in Fig. 2(b). The vibration of CC stretching is represented by the peak at 1591 cm−1, which indicates the presence of sp2 carbon, that is in charge of CQD core conjugation [18].

The results of FE-SEM characterization, shown in Fig. 3 (a-b), showed how ZnO morphology changed when CQDs were added. Adhesion between ZnO and CQDs is evident and this led in an irregular nanoflower shape with a rougher surface [18]. In terms of mass fraction and atomic mass percentage, ZnO and CQDs are confirmed to be present by the elemental distribution mapping shown in Fig. 3(c–e) and the EDX spectra provided in Table SI-2. ZnO/CQDs were formed by heterogeneous small ZnO primer particles with a size of 20 nm, according to the HR-TEM image (Fig. 3(f)). The lattice fringe of ZnO is 0.28 nm and CQD is 0.31 nm, were distributed uniformly on the surface of ZnO/CQD. It can be determined that ZnO/CQD was successfully prepared as a photocatalyst material through the green synthesize method.

The hysteresis loop in Fig. 4 (a-c) indicates that the photocatalytic materials show type IV adsorption/desorption isotherms. ZnO/CQDs have a greater surface area than ZnO. According to Widiyandari et al., [19] adsorption can be improved by a greater surface area of materials. Based on Table SI-3, all samples are also classified as mesoporous materials with pore sizes among 2 and 50 nm.

The increasing in surface area and defect concentration on ZnO/CQDs made the ZnO exciton PL intensity diminishes. This has been hypothesized to be a consequence of excited electrons and holes recombining at the band edge. Consequently, under typical conditions, a bulk semiconductor material may show no PL signal at all or only a weak PL signal. The green emission seen at the peak of the curve in Fig. 4(d) show that the emission ranges from 560 to 565 nm, so that the recombination rate of electron-hole pairs emitted by light corresponds precisely with the intensity of photoluminescence emission [19]. Reduced electron-hole recombination, which may inhibit electron recombination, is suggested by the peak's decrease in ZnO/CQDs [19].

The graphical representation in Fig. 4(e) clearly shows that the bandgap energy of ZnO is 3.22 eV, whereas it is slightly lower at 3.18 eV for ZnO/CQDs. This is caused by CQDs, which have nanoscale (<10 nm) dimensions and create a dense hetero-nanostructure. This reduces the time and distance required for charge transfer, speeds up charge transfer at the interface, and may be able to expand the light absorption spectrum because of their lower energy [4,18].

Fig. 5(a) shows how in comparison to ZnO, the MB concentration on ZnO/CQDs decreases more quickly with the degradation percentages of MB without photocatalyst, ZnO, and ZnO/CQDs were 16.37%, 97.2%, and 98.5% respectively with the same irradiation time. As seen in Fig. 5(b), the data are consistent with the Langmuir-Hinshelwood kinetic model. The degradation of the MB takes place through the first-order reaction. The linear regression values and reaction rate constants for photocatalytic materials are shown in Table SI-4. According to the results, CQDs added to ZnO could accelerate the MB solution's degradation Fig. 5(e). It proved that the composited of CQDs to ZnO increases the photocatalytic activity. ZnO/CQDs as photocatalyst materials can degrade the MB solution so that the final solution is colorless and ZnO/CQDs photocatalyst material degrades methylene blue more quickly than ZnO. In comparison (Fig. 5(c-d)), ZnO and ZnO/CQDs as photocatalyst materials also succeeded in degrading ciprofloxacin more quickly. From the results shown within 80 min, ZnO only degraded 31%, while ZnO/CQDs indicated an improved photocatalytic performance (46% degradation).

The decrease in MB concentration with each subsequent cycle is shown in Fig. 6(a). The concentration of the MB solution successfully drops until the fourth cycle, suggesting that the sample is suitable for further reactions. According to Fig. 6(b), the ZnO/CQD photocatalyst continuously produces stable results after each cycle of repetition. These results suggest that the photocatalyst ZnO/CQDs can be used repeatedly to degrade methylene blue compounds through photocatalysis.

The trapping experiment by using various trapping agents has been studied to evaluate the active species in the MB degradation. Fig. 7(a) shows the degradation percentage of MB under visible light irradiation in the absence of scavengers (none) and in the presence of multiple scavengers with ZnO/CQDs photocatalysts. For each scavenger, IPA as hydroxyl (·OH) radical scavenger, AO as a hole (h+) scavenger, and BQ as an ·O2 − scavenger [20]. The estimated photodegradation percentages and outcomes of the scavenger test were 38%, 82%, and 76%, respectively. The photocatalytic degradation of MB enhanced significantly upon the addition of IPA as an ·OH scavenger. These results suggest that ·OH radicals primarily cause MB degradation under UV–vis irradiation, while ·O2 − radicals have a minor effect on the process [21].

Depending on the previously discussed issues, we considered a possible mechanism for the ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites' visible light-driven photodegradation process, as shown in Fig. 7(b). When visible and irradiation light are radiated on the surface of ZnO, photogenerated holes will absorb oxygen from the air and convert it into strong active substances like ·OH, thus removing the pollutant. Furthermore, photogenerated electrons will produce a transition from the visible to the conduction band [22]. In addition, the long wavelength light from visible light may be shifted to shorter wavelength light that ZnO can use because of the up-conversion property of CQDs. Also, under light circumstances, CQDs may absorb photogenerated electrons from ZnO and react with oxygen on its surface to produce oxygen free radicals, which further degrades the dye in order to achieve the photocatalytic activity purpose.

The synthesis was carried out using a green and environmentally safe approach, using the hydrothermal method in order to use lime as a precursor. After 30 min, the ZnO/CQDs composite material showed remarkable photocatalytic activity, degrading Methylene Blue with an outstanding efficiency of around 91%. This indicates that ZnO and lime-derived CQDs significantly enhance each other's photocatalytic performance, highlighting this compound's potential for effective pollution removal in applications using visible light.

Rizky Nur Hidayat: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Hendri Widiyandari: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization. Hanaiyah Parasdila: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Orien Prilita: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Investigation. Yayuk Astuti: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Nandang Mufti: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Takashi Ogi: Writing – review & editing, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by Ministry of Education, Culture, Research, and Technology Republic of Indonesia under the grant of Riset Kolaborasi Indonesia with contract number: 590.1/UN27.22/ HK.07.00/2023. The Author (HW) thanks to Osi Arutanti (National Research and Innovation Agency) for providing HR-TEM characterization.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.catcom.2024.106888.
